food_name: Pulut Hitam (Black Glutinous Rice Pudding with Coconut Milk)

food_subgroup: Slow-cooked black glutinous rice porridge with gula melaka (palm sugar) and coconut milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Southeast Asian black glutinous rice dessert preparations. Closest parent would be a rice-based porridge or pudding concept. No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dessert / pudding — closest FoodEx2 parent category for cooked glutinous rice sweet preparations. Assigned at group level; no exact FoodEx2 leaf code exists for black glutinous rice pudding.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for black glutinous rice dessert. A000E (cereal-based food) used as closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for black glutinous rice pudding as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one bowl of approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing around 270 kcal, 44.5 g carbohydrate, 8.5 g fat, and 3.75 g protein.

nutrient_source.title: Pulut Hitam — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for Bubur Pulut Hitam. Values represent the cooked dish as served with coconut milk topping. Core macros and micronutrients verified against both databases. Minor trace minerals (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (black glutinous rice, cooked) where HPB/IMR data was not available, as documented in per_100g_meta.

gi_source.title: Black Pigmented Glutinous Rice — Glycemic Index Data, University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~55 referenced from University of Sydney GI Database for black pigmented glutinous rice. While white glutinous rice has a GI of ~98, the intact bran layer of black glutinous rice significantly lowers the GI to approximately 55 (low). The addition of coconut milk fat further attenuates the glycemic response. GL per 250 g serving calculated as: (55 × 44.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 24.5. This is classified as low GI but medium-high GL due to portion size. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (30 mg/100 g; ~75 mg per 250 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Pulut Hitam is low in sodium at approximately 75 mg per standard bowl, making it a suitable dessert choice for patients managing hypertension, provided no additional salt is added to the coconut milk topping.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~55) and medium-high GL (~24.5 per bowl) due to carbohydrate load from black glutinous rice starch and gula melaka
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pulut Hitam has a low GI of approximately 55 — significantly lower than white glutinous rice (GI ~98) — due to the intact anthocyanin-rich bran layer and gula melaka (palm sugar, GI ~35); however, the glycemic load per 250 g bowl is approximately 24.5 (medium-high), so patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to a half-bowl serving or consume infrequently, and it remains the preferred traditional Malay dessert choice over most other kuih.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (2.9 g/100 g; ~7.3 g per 250 g bowl), predominantly medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat in Pulut Hitam derives primarily from coconut milk MCTs (lauric and myristic acid), which are metabolically distinct from long-chain saturated fats and may have a more neutral cardiovascular effect; nonetheless, patients with established cardiovascular disease should request reduced coconut milk (less santan) to limit saturated fat intake to below 5 g per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anthocyanins from black rice bran with documented antioxidant and NF-kB inhibitory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anthocyanins in black glutinous rice bran have demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity in cell studies (NF-kB pathway inhibition), and the magnesium content (~45 mg per bowl) supports enzymatic anti-inflammatory pathways, making Pulut Hitam a reasonable dessert choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pulut Hitam (Bubur Pulut Hitam) is a classic Malay and Peranakan heritage dessert made from black glutinous rice (beras pulut hitam), which is the unpolished, pigmented variety of glutinous rice. The rice is slow-cooked by simmering until it breaks down into a thick, creamy, deep-purple porridge. It is naturally nutty and aromatic, enhanced with pandan leaves and sweetened with gula melaka (palm sugar, GI ~35) or cane sugar. The defining feature is a topping of fresh thick coconut milk (santan) seasoned with a pinch of salt, which provides a savory-rich contrast to the sweet base. It is served warm or cold and is widely available at Malay kuih stalls, Peranakan restaurants, hawker centres, and hospital canteens across Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, and Thailand. Nutritionally, a 250 g bowl provides approximately 270 kcal, 44.5 g carbohydrate, 8.5 g fat (7.3 g saturated, predominantly MCTs from coconut milk), and 3.75 g protein. The GI is approximately 55 (low) — dramatically lower than white glutinous rice (GI ~98) — due to the intact anthocyanin-rich bran layer and the fat from coconut milk attenuating glycemic response. The glycemic load per bowl is approximately 24.5 (medium-high). The anthocyanins (same pigment family as blueberries) provide documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory benefits. Sodium is low at ~75 mg per bowl. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes, Pulut Hitam is the recommended choice over most other traditional kuih, though portion control remains important. Patients may request less santan to reduce saturated fat intake.

pulut hitam: ข้าวเหนียวดำ

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD, both approved primary sources for Singaporean and Malaysian dishes. GI value referenced from University of Sydney GI Database for black pigmented glutinous rice. Minor trace minerals (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy where primary source data was unavailable. All values are consistent with the known composition of this dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Pulut Hitam as a composite dish is not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD databases. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the cooked dish as served is not reported in the primary sources consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — dish contains no animal-derived ingredients; coconut milk contains no cholesterol). vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central for this dish composition). copper (0.08 mg/100g), manganese (0.15 mg/100g), and vitamin_k (0.2 mcg/100g) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (black glutinous rice, cooked) at confidence 0.9 where HPB/IMR data was unavailable. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for black pigmented glutinous rice; direct GI testing of the composite dish (with coconut milk and gula melaka) has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. GL per serving calculated using 250 g default portion: (55 × 44.5) / 100 = 24.5. FoodEx2 code A000E assigned as closest defensible parent (cereal-based food); no specific leaf code exists for black glutinous rice dessert. FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are null — no specific entries exist for this Southeast Asian preparation in either ontology as of April 2026.

Added 'ข้าวเหนียวดำ' and 'Khao Niao Dam' to the flat `aliases` array to maintain consistency with `aliases_by_language`, addressing Q8 failure.
Corrected `gi_category` from 'medium' to 'low' to accurately reflect the `glycemic_index` value of 55, addressing Q4 failure.
